name: Anki / FSRS
overview: Anki is the de-facto open-source spaced repetition system, used heavily by language learners and medical students for almost two decades. Since version 23.10 (Nov 2023), Anki ships with FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) by Jarrett Ye, replacing the legacy SM-2 algorithm. FSRS uses the DSR (Difficulty / Stability / Retrievability) memory model and per-user machine-learning optimization to schedule reviews at a configurable target retention (default 90%), reducing review workload by 20-30% versus SM-2 at equal retention.
originator_or_company: Anki: Damien Elmes (Australia, 2006), now transitioning stewardship to AnkiHub (announced 2026). FSRS: Jarrett Ye (creator, Research Engineer at MaiMemo Inc.; founder of Thoughts Memo). Algorithm developed within the open-spaced-repetition GitHub org.
key_resources: [Anki Manual - What spaced repetition algorithm does Anki use?](https://faqs.ankiweb.net/what-spaced-repetition-algorithm)
[Jarrett Ye - The History of FSRS for Anki (LessWrong)](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/G7fpGCi8r7nCKXsQk/the-history-of-fsrs-for-anki)
[Domenic Denicola - Spaced Repetition Systems Have Gotten Way Better](https://domenic.me/fsrs/)
core_mechanics: FSRS models each card with three latent variables under the DSR model: (1) Retrievability R in [0,1], the probability of recalling the card now, decaying via R(t) = (1 + F * t/S)^C where F and C are constants making R = 0.9 when t = S; (2) Stability S in days, the time required for R to drop from 100% to 90%; (3) Difficulty D in [1,10], a per-card hardness factor. After each review, the user grades the card on a 4-button scale (Again / Hard / Good / Easy); FSRS-5 / FSRS-6 use 19-21 trained parameters (w0..w20) to update S and D from the grade, the elapsed interval, and the previous (S, D, R). The next interval is computed by inverting the R formula at the user-set Desired Retention (default 90%), so harder cards reappear sooner and easier cards drift out to months or years. Parameters are first initialized from a population fit over ~700M reviews from 20k users, then re-optimized per-user via gradient descent on log-loss over their own review history (the Optimize button in Anki). Same-day reviews use a separate short-term update path. Anki UI mechanics on top: FSRS desk + Again/Hard/Good/Easy buttons, automatic interval display, Stats tab showing retention/load forecast, Deck Options for Desired Retention and Maximum Interval, Custom Study, Filtered Decks, and a heatmap calendar.
designer_or_researcher_voice: Jarrett Ye (LessWrong, History of FSRS): 'That comment really rubbed me the wrong way. So, determined to prove them wrong, I decided to implement the FSRS algorithm within Anki.'

Jarrett Ye on the ACM SIGKDD acceptance: 'For me, this was undoubtedly the most exciting news, as it meant the most popular open-source spaced repetition software would potentially use the algorithm I had researched and developed.'

Jarrett Ye on FSRS maturity: 'At the time, I believed this would be the final version of FSRS. This was because community members and I had subsequently attempted dozens more improvement methods, all of which, without exception, proved ineffective.'
Jarrett Ye on the model's limits: 'The key lesson learned from trying to predict short-term memory with FSRS was that the working mechanisms of short-term and long-term memory are quite different.'
Anki FAQ (official): 'You can expect to do 20-30% fewer reviews to achieve the same level of knowledge retention when using FSRS.'

Matt vs Japan (X, summarising the change): 'In November 2023, Anki officially adopted the new FSRS algorithm... You'll now have 20-30% fewer reviews, yet achieve the same retention you had with the old algorithm.'
metrics_or_evidence: Workload reduction: 20-30% fewer reviews vs. SM-2 at equal retention (Anki official FAQ; benchmarked on the open-spaced-repetition srs-benchmark dataset). Default population parameters trained on ~700M reviews from ~20k Anki users. FSRS-5 (released 2024-07-10) reduced log-loss prediction error by ~4% over FSRS-4.5; recency weighting (2024-12-30) added another ~4.5%. FSRS-6 reaches ~83.3% superiority over SM-17 across the SuperMemo benchmark (Expertium / Jarrett Ye). Configurable Desired Retention (default 90%, recommended range 80-95%). Adoption among medical students: Anki use rates of 60-94% in cohort studies (PMC10403443, PMC12662189), with Anki users averaging ~74% of total study time on the app. No public DAU/MAU figures are released by Ankitects.
criticisms: Burnout is the most cited issue: spaced repetition is mathematically uneven, so daily review counts spike unpredictably and large backlogs after a break feel demoralizing (Anki Forums #7502; reddit r/Anki, r/medicalschool). Critics including polyglot Luca Lampariello argue Anki strips vocabulary/concepts of context, encouraging recognition without production. The leech and ease-hell problems of legacy SM-2 are partially fixed by FSRS but reset/optimization workflows remain confusing. FSRS itself is harder to explain than SM-2 ('parameters' instead of 'ease factor'), which makes user trust harder to build. Short-term memory and re-learning are not modeled as well as long-term retention (Jarrett Ye, LessWrong). Population defaults assume the average user is similar to the AnkiWeb training set, which skews toward language and medical learners.
failure_cases: Documented user-reported failures: med students reporting 1671-review backlogs and quitting (Anki Forums); USMLE Step 1 students burning out on 70+ new cards/day taking 3+ hours of daily reviews; ~80% of newcomers reportedly abandon SRS within months (My Senpai 2024 survey citing reddit data); add-card addiction cycle where users add far more cards than they can sustain reviewing. Apple Watch / iPhone Anki Mobile pricing ($24.99 one-time) has been a steady source of complaints in App Store reviews. The legacy SM-2 'ease factor' downward spiral ('ease hell') was a structural failure mode that drove the FSRS migration. Same-day learning step heuristics still confuse users moving from SM-2 presets to FSRS presets.
antipatterns: Do not surface raw review backlog counts as the primary daily metric - it triggers avoidance and quitting. Do not let users add cards faster than the schedule can absorb without a clear cap or warning. Do not expose w0..w20 parameter sliders to a casual learner audience; FSRS in Anki Desktop already shows these and it confuses non-technical users. Do not gamify the Again button (penalty animations / streak loss) - it incentivises lying about recall, which corrupts the DSR fit. Do not force-publish a Desired Retention number; framing 90% as a 'goal you are failing' is a Means-Ends Reversal. Avoid push notifications that nag every overdue card - this is the single biggest driver of Anki uninstalls in qualitative reports.
applicability_to_shikaq: Highly applicable as the core memory engine for Practice. Use FSRS (via the open-source rs-fsrs / fsrs.js port) as the scheduler behind shikaq's question bank, with a 90% Desired Retention default and an 'exam date mode' that auto-tunes retention upward as the test approaches. HEXAD mapping: serves Achievers (mastery progression), Philanthropists (the science-backed 'right thing'), and Free Spirits (autonomy via configurable retention). For the 20s-female / cafe / iPad persona, hide all FSRS internals - never show 'parameters' or 'log loss'. Surface only: today's queue size (capped), a Fitness-style 3-ring (Reviewed / Accuracy / Streak), a Books-style shelf of certifications, and a single tasteful 'You'll remember this until <date>' line on the answer screen, borrowed from RemNote/Mochi. Pair with shikaq's existing Practice screen (CBT mode = scheduled FSRS queue) and Summary screen (retention forecast graph in Apple Fitness style). Use Liquid Glass tab bar for the review session toolbar (Again/Hard/Good/Easy) to keep content primary. Optional: an 'Aozora Mode' that caps daily reviews to e.g. 30 to specifically address the burnout antipattern - critical for the cafe-study persona who will not tolerate a 200-card backlog.
phase_assignment: Phase 1 (3 weeks). FSRS is the foundational memory engine; everything else (streaks, rings, AI Q&A, audio quiz) layers on top of the question schedule. Without FSRS, the Practice tab is a generic quiz app. Adopting an existing port (rs-fsrs, py-fsrs, ts-fsrs) keeps Phase 1 implementation cost low.
integration_points: DB: extend `questions` and add a new `card_states` table (user_id, question_id, stability, difficulty, last_review_at, next_due_at, scheduled_days, reps, lapses, state). Scheduling logic in a Supabase Edge Function or client-side ts-fsrs running on the device with sync via `quiz_results`. Components: Practice screen (renders due queue from card_states), Summary screen / Apple Fitness rings (read aggregated retention forecast), TodayContent (shows today's due count + 'remember by' chip), Sidebar (Practice badge for due count, capped). Re-uses existing `quiz_results` table for review history -> nightly Edge Function runs FSRS optimizer per user once they have >=200 reviews. RLS: card_states owned by user_id, admin bypass for analytics.
risks: Implementation: the Anki Desktop FSRS UI exposes raw parameters and confuses users; shikaq must aggressively hide complexity, which costs design effort. Optimizer: per-user parameter optimization needs >=200 reviews to be meaningful; cold-start UX must use population defaults gracefully. Ethical: FSRS-driven daily queues can spike and feel oppressive on cafe-study days - mitigate with a daily cap and a 'pause without backlog' mode. Persona fit: hardcore Anki-style 'review or die' framing is incompatible with the 20s-female casual cafe persona; needs full design re-skin. Scope creep: full FSRS-6 + custom optimizer is overkill for Phase 1 - stick to a vetted port at default parameters. Licensing: FSRS code is BSD/MIT-style permissive (open-spaced-repetition org) so no GPL contamination, but verify each port's license before bundling.
apple_hig_compatibility: compatible. FSRS itself is invisible math, so the rendering is purely a HIG question. The four-button grade (Again/Hard/Good/Easy) maps cleanly to a horizontal Liquid Glass toolbar like Apple Music's playback controls. Retention forecast = Apple Fitness 3-ring. Daily queue = Apple News+ Featured card. Streak = SF Symbol `flame.fill`. The only HIG-conflicting pattern in upstream Anki Desktop (parameter sliders, dense statistics tables) must be omitted; ship the polished consumer surface only.
ethical_score: positive. FSRS is one of the few mainstream consumer algorithms whose optimization target is genuinely user-serving (long-term retention, fewer reviews) rather than session length or ad inventory. Risks of burnout are real but stem from how products *present* the queue, not the algorithm. shikaq can amplify the positive by capping daily review load, never penalizing skipped days, and framing retention as a forecast rather than a quota. No dependency loop, no variable reward schedule, no time-after-time-regret pattern.
evidence_quality: rigorous_RCT for the underlying spacing effect (Cepeda 2008 meta-analysis, Karpicke testing-effect work). For FSRS specifically: large-scale observational benchmarks (open-spaced-repetition srs-benchmark, ACM SIGKDD paper from MaiMemo). Anki-in-medical-school: multiple cohort studies in PMC (observational, not randomized). FSRS vs SM-2 workload claim is based on benchmark log-loss + simulation, not a head-to-head RCT.
localization_fit_japan: high. Anki itself was originally built by Damien Elmes for learning Japanese and has a long Japanese user base (Wanikani / Migaku / Matt vs Japan crowd). FSRS has Japanese-language community discussion on Twitter/X and Zenn. The 20s-female cafe-study persona will not engage with raw Anki, but the underlying scheduling is culturally invisible - what matters is the wrapper. Japanese exam culture (TOEIC, 簿記, ITパスポート) has strong demand for 'efficient repetition without wasting commute time', which is precisely the FSRS pitch. Shikaq's iPad-cafe framing fits cleanly.
